author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
review: A dazzling portrait of the Jazz Age, 'The Great Gatsby' follows the mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby and his obsessive pursuit of the lost love Daisy Buchanan. Through Nick Carraway's eyes, Fitzgerald critiques the hollow excess of the American Dream, weaving a tragic tale of longing, wealth, and betrayal. The prose is lyrical and haunting, making this slim novel an enduring masterpiece about the green light we all chase.

author: Harper Lee
review: Through the eyes of young Scout Finch, Harper Lee's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel explores racial injustice and moral growth in the Deep South. Atticus Finch, a lawyer defending a Black man falsely accused of rape, stands as a beacon of integrity. Heartbreaking and wise, this story teaches empathy, courage, and the importance of standing up for what is right, even when the world opposes you.

author: George Orwell
review: Orwell's terrifying vision of totalitarianism remains a stark warning for every generation. In a world of Big Brother, thought control, and perpetual war, Winston Smith dares to seek truth and love. The concepts of Newspeak, doublethink, and the ever-watching telescreen have entered our cultural vocabulary. Dark, claustrophobic, and unforgettable, '1984' forces us to question power, surveillance, and the fragility of memory.

author: J.D. Salinger
review: Holden Caulfield, the original angsty teenager, narrates his rebellious journey through New York City after being expelled from prep school. Salinger captures adolescent alienation, grief, and the fear of adulthood with raw honesty. Holden's desire to be the 'catcher in the rye'—protecting innocent children from falling—is both heartbreaking and hopeful. A controversial classic that speaks to anyone who has ever felt out of place.

author: Jane Austen
review: Austen's sparkling comedy of manners follows the witty Elizabeth Bennet as she navigates family pressure, social class, and her own misjudgments. Her clash with the proud Mr. Darcy is one of literature's greatest slow-burn romances. Filled with sharp satire, unforgettable characters (including the absurd Mr. Collins), and a timeless examination of love, reputation, and integrity, this novel remains as fresh and delightful as ever.

author: J.R.R. Tolkien
review: Bilbo Baggins, a comfort-loving hobbit, is swept into an epic adventure by the wizard Gandalf and a company of dwarves. Their quest to reclaim the Lonely Mountain from the dragon Smaug introduces readers to the rich world of Middle-earth. With riddles in the dark with Gollum, encounters with giant spiders, and the finding of a mysterious ring, Tolkien crafts a perfect blend of heroism, humor, and wonder. A timeless fantasy classic for all ages.

bookName: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone
author: J.K. Rowling
review: The book that started a global phenomenon. Harry Potter discovers he is a wizard on his 11th birthday and enters Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry. From the magic of Diagon Alley to the thrill of Quidditch and the mystery of the Sorcerer's Stone, Rowling builds a world of wonder, friendship, and courage. Themes of love, loyalty, and the battle against prejudice shine through this enchanting and heartfelt debut.

author: Paulo Coelho
review: This philosophical fable follows Santiago, a young Andalusian shepherd, who dreams of a treasure near the Egyptian pyramids. His journey across the desert teaches him to listen to his heart, read omens, and understand the 'Soul of the World.' Coelho's simple, allegorical prose delivers powerful lessons about following one's 'Personal Legend' and the idea that the treasure we seek often lies where we began. Inspiring and meditative.

author: Paula Hawkins
review: A gripping psychological thriller told through the unreliable narration of Rachel, an alcoholic commuter who becomes obsessed with a couple she watches from the train window. When the woman goes missing, Rachel inserts herself into the investigation, but her fractured memory and questionable judgment blur the line between truth and fantasy. Twisty, dark, and compulsively readable, this modern noir explores memory, gaslighting, and the masks we wear.
